metadata.summary: Route LLM calls through a gateway with fallback chain across providers. On provider 5xx, 429, timeout, transparently retry next model. Better availability than single vendor.

content.title: Cost-Aware Gateway with Fallback Chain (OpenRouter Pattern)
content.summary: Do not hard-code a single model in production agent code. Send each call to a gateway (OpenRouter, LiteLLM proxy, Portkey, Kong AI Gateway) that exposes a primary model plus an ordered fallback chain; on provider 5xx, 429, or timeout the gateway transparently retries the next model in the list. Bill only the successful run. The result: better availability than any single vendor, no code change to swap models, and a single integration surface for prompt-caching, retries, observability, and budget caps.

why.p: Single-vendor agents fail closed: a 30-minute Anthropic outage is a 30-minute product outage. Multi-provider gateways turn vendor incidents into degraded-but-running scenarios. OpenRouter's Zero Completion Insurance and ordered fallbacks have been observed to keep tail availability above 99.95% while individual providers run at 99.5-99.9%. The economic side is equally strong: per-call routing across providers lets the gateway pick the cheapest model that meets the quality bar, and the fallback chain absorbs rate limits during traffic spikes without code change. Direct vendor SDK calls give up all of that for a marginal latency win.

list.item: Production agents with an availability SLA above the single-vendor floor.
Teams running A/B experiments across providers (Anthropic vs OpenAI vs Gemini) without code changes.
Workloads that hit rate limits on a single provider during peak hours.
Multi-region deployments where the cheapest provider differs per region.
Strict data-residency or compliance regimes (EU healthcare, defense) where the gateway is a third-party processor — go direct, with a controlled fallback inside the same boundary.
Pipelines that depend on raw vendor SDK features the gateway does not expose (Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI Batch API, fine-tuned models).
Local development and experimentation — the gateway adds latency and operational surface for no production benefit.
Edge / on-device inference — gateway round-trip dominates total latency.

section.name: fallback-semantics
section.p: The gateway accepts a primary model plus an ordered list of alternates. On a fallback-eligible error from the primary (5xx, 429, timeout, content-policy refusal, or empty completion), it transparently retries the next model in the list with the same messages. Only the successful call is billed.

rule.statement: Configure every production LLM call with a primary model AND at least one fallback model from a different provider; same-vendor fallbacks do not protect against vendor-wide incidents.
rule.rationale: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google have all had multi-hour outages affecting all of their models simultaneously; cross-provider fallbacks are the only path to availability above the single-vendor floor.

example.title: Cross-provider fallback chain
example.code: client.chat.completions.create( model="anthropic/claude-opus-4", messages=messages, extra_body={"models": [ "anthropic/claude-opus-4", # primary "openai/gpt-5", # cross-provider fallback "google/gemini-2.5-pro", # second fallback ]}, )
example.note: Three providers, ordered by quality; Anthropic outage routes to OpenAI; OpenAI rate limit routes to Gemini.

example.title: Same-vendor fallbacks
example.note: All three depend on Anthropic infrastructure; a vendor-wide outage takes them all down at once.

rule.statement: Surface 4xx client errors (400 bad request, 401 unauthenticated, 422 validation) without fallback; these are agent bugs, not provider incidents, and silently retrying masks them.
rule.rationale: Falling back on a 400 means you ship a broken request to every provider in the chain, multiplying the failure surface and hiding the bug from observability.

antipattern.description: Fallback chain with the same model from three different routers (e.g., direct, OpenRouter, AWS Bedrock).
antipattern.reason: Same model means same content-policy refusal and same outage class; you pay three integrations for one logical fallback.

section.name: fallback-triggers
section.p: The gateway retries on specific error classes. Understanding what counts as a fallback trigger is critical to prevent masking real bugs.

list.item: HTTP 5xx from the upstream provider — retry next.
HTTP 429 rate limit — retry next, optionally after a short backoff if no other provider is configured.
Network timeout (default 60s) — retry next with the same message body.
Empty completion or content-policy refusal — retry next ONLY if the policy on this path says so; otherwise surface the refusal.

section.name: compliance-and-data-residency
section.p: The gateway becomes a third-party data processor. For regulated workloads (HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2 Type II, defense), the gateway must either be self-hosted (LiteLLM proxy, Kong AI Gateway) inside the same compliance boundary as the application, or excluded entirely.

rule.statement: If the workload is subject to data-residency or compliance constraints, either self-host the gateway inside the same boundary or call the provider SDK directly and implement the fallback chain in application code.
rule.rationale: Hosted gateways such as OpenRouter route data through their own infrastructure; this can break data-residency contracts and add a third-party processor to the audit scope.

section.name: vendor-feature-parity
section.p: Gateways trail vendor SDKs on cutting-edge features. Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI Batch API, Gemini long-context, and per-vendor fine-tunes typically take weeks to land in any gateway after release.

rule.statement: For any workload that depends on a vendor-specific feature (prompt caching, batch API, fine-tunes), call the vendor SDK directly and implement the fallback chain at the application layer; do not route those calls through a gateway.
rule.rationale: The cost win from prompt caching (~90% on cached input) is far larger than the cost win from gateway routing for those workloads; routing through the gateway typically loses the cache.

example.title: High-volume cached workload — direct Anthropic, app-level fallback
example.note: Direct call preserves prompt caching; fallback chain still runs in app code on real failures.

antipattern.description: Routing every call through the gateway "for consistency" while paying for prompt-caching workloads.
antipattern.reason: Loses the cache discount on the workloads that benefit from it the most; the gateway saving is dwarfed by the cache loss.

section.name: observability-and-billing
section.p: The gateway is a natural choke-point for telemetry: every call gets a trace, a cost record, and a per-model success-rate metric. Bill only successful runs (Zero Completion Insurance) so failed fallbacks do not show up on the invoice.

rule.statement: Emit OTel GenAI spans for every gateway call with attributes for primary_model, used_model, fallback_count, and cost_usd; alert when fallback_count rises above a baseline.
rule.rationale: Rising fallback rate is the leading indicator of vendor incidents and quota exhaustion; without per-call telemetry the first signal is angry users.

template.purpose: OpenRouter chat-completions call with a primary model and an explicit fallback chain.
